name: lipar-training-free-video-pruning
description: Accelerate video generation by exploiting temporal redundancy through training-free pruning of duplicate latent patches. Recover visual quality using attention approximation and noise-aware duplication to achieve 1.45x speedup without training modifications or quality loss.
paperTitle: Training-free Latent Inter-Frame Pruning with Attention Recovery
content:
## Problem

Video generation models recompute identical latent patches across frames despite strong temporal coherence, wasting 45-55% of computation on redundant calculations. Training-free acceleration methods preserve quality while exploiting this redundancy, but standard token pruning breaks attention mechanisms, requiring careful recovery strategies.

## Actionable Technique: Latent Inter-Frame Pruning

Identify and skip recomputing duplicate latent patches by comparing consecutive frame representations:

```python
# Training-free temporal pruning
class LatentInterFramePruning:
    def __init__(self, threshold_τ1=0.15, threshold_τ2=0.30):
        self.τ1 = threshold_τ1  # Hard threshold for motion detection
        self.τ2 = threshold_τ2  # Soft threshold for boundary regions

    def identify_static_patches(self, frame_t, frame_t1):
        """Identify patches unchanged between frames."""
        # Compute per-patch temporal difference
        patch_diff = torch.norm(frame_t - frame_t1, dim=-1)

        # Binary mask: static if diff < threshold
        static_mask = patch_diff < self.τ1

        return static_mask

    def apply_motion_aware_filtering(self, static_mask):
        """Prevent false pruning in motion regions."""
        # Apply 3D Gaussian adaptive thresholding
        filtered_mask = gaussian_adaptive_threshold(
            static_mask,
            sigma_spatial=2.0,
            sigma_temporal=1.5
        )

        # Morphological operations: dilate true negatives
        # This prevents disconnected motion regions
        filtered_mask = binary_dilation(filtered_mask, iterations=1)

        return filtered_mask

    def prune_static_patches(self, features, static_mask):
        """Extract only dynamic patches for processing."""
        dynamic_indices = torch.where(~static_mask)
        dynamic_features = features[dynamic_indices]

        return dynamic_features, dynamic_indices
```

Empirically, 0.69-0.77 Pearson correlation between pixel and latent temporal changes validates this approach.

## Actionable Technique: Attention Recovery via M-Degree Approximation

Preserve attention output quality using only m most relevant tokens:

```python
# Attention approximation for pruned tokens
class AttentionRecovery:
    def __init__(self, rank_m=8):
        self.rank_m = rank_m

    def recover_attention_output(self, query_features, retained_kv_cache, pruned_positions):
        """Approximate attention output for pruned tokens."""
        # Standard attention on retained tokens
        attention_weights = torch.softmax(
            torch.mm(query_features, retained_kv_cache.t()) / math.sqrt(dim),
            dim=-1
        )

        # Select top-m positions: use rotational position encoding
        top_m_positions = select_top_m_by_position_encoding(
            retained_kv_cache, self.rank_m
        )

        # Approximate attention: only consider top-m positions
        # This reduces O(n²) to O(n·m) for pruned computation
        approximate_weights = attention_weights[:, top_m_positions]
        approximate_weights = approximate_weights / approximate_weights.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True)

        # Reconstruct attention output
        attention_output = torch.mm(
            approximate_weights,
            retained_kv_cache[top_m_positions]
        )

        return attention_output
```

## Actionable Technique: Noise-Aware KV-Cache Duplication

Preserve noise properties when reusing cached tokens:

```python
# Noise-aware cache management
def duplicate_kv_cache_noise_aware(kv_cache_previous, current_denoising_step):
    """Reuse KV cache while preserving i.i.d. noise assumptions."""
    # KV cache has deterministic component (from model) + stochastic noise
    # Reusing stale cache breaks i.i.d. assumption

    # Solution: Duplicate only clean token components
    clean_component = kv_cache_previous  # Deterministic part
    noise_component = sample_noise(kv_cache_previous.shape)  # Fresh noise

    # Combine: preserve signal, refresh noise
    refreshed_kv_cache = (
        clean_component +
        noise_level(current_denoising_step) * noise_component
    )

    return refreshed_kv_cache
```

This maintains attention properties while preventing stale noise accumulation.

## Actionable Technique: Dual-Term Masking for Attention Recovery

Combine short-term and long-term temporal differences for robust pruning decisions:

```python
# Dual-term masking strategy
def compute_dual_term_mask(frame_sequence):
    """Balance immediate and contextual motion."""
    # Short-term: consecutive frame differences
    short_term = torch.norm(
        frame_sequence[1:] - frame_sequence[:-1],
        dim=-1
    )

    # Long-term: difference from frame 4 steps back
    long_term = torch.norm(
        frame_sequence[4:] - frame_sequence[:-4],
        dim=-1
    )

    # Combine: conservative decision requires motion in both terms
    # OR aggressive requires motion in either term (task-dependent)
    combined_mask = (short_term < τ1) & (long_term < τ2)  # Conservative
    # combined_mask = (short_term < τ1) | (long_term < τ2)  # Aggressive

    return combined_mask
```

## When NOT to use

- Very fast motion (sports, action) where inter-frame changes extreme
- Flickering/noise-heavy video where temporal stability unreliable
- Real-time inference where latency of pruning detection adds overhead

## Performance Metrics

| Metric | Baseline | LIPAR | Improvement |
|--------|----------|-------|-------------|
| Throughput (FPS) | 8.4 | 12.2 | 1.45× |
| Visual quality (FID) | 16.2 | 16.3 | -0.06% |
| Pruning ratio | - | 0.42 | 42% tokens pruned |
| Human preference | 50% | 86.4% win | Baseline inferior |
